coredata.dc:title: Fluorescence/colorimetry/smartphone triple-mode sensing platform based on carbon dots and Co, Fe-layered double hydroxides for citrate determination
coredata.dc:creator: Yin, Donghang
Zheng, Shujun
Xu, Xuechao
Wang, Fei
Wang, Linjie
coredata.dc:description: The cheap, simple, but reliable non-invasive detection means and mature early warning mechanism are beneficial for male population in underdeveloped regions to get rid of the physical and mental pain caused by prostate cancer (PCa). Citrate is presently regarded as a potential biomarker for early non-invasive diagnosis of PCa due to significant changes of citrate levels in urine during the development of PCa, even in the early stages. In this work, the Co, Fe doped layered double hydroxide (CoFe-LDH) can significantly quench the fluorescence of carbon dots (CDs) via inner filter effect (IFE) and catalyze the oxidation of colorless TMB to generate blue TMBox because of excellent oxidase-mimetic activity. However, the introduction of citrate can form a stable complex with CoFe-LDH via intercalation effect, thereby restoring the fluorescence of CDs and inhibition of oxidase-like activity of CoFe-LDH. Based on this, a triple-mode sensing platform (fluorescence, colorimetry, and smartphone) based on CDs + CoFe-LDH system has been fabricated for citrate analysis with correlative detection limits of 0.35, 0.42, and 0.60 μM. Furthermore, these three modes are applied to citrate analysis in male urine samples with ideal recoveries ranging from 94.4 % to 107.4 %, demonstrate the reliability and stability of these triple-mode sensing platform in practical applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Prostate cancer
Citrate
CoFe-LDH
Oxidase-like activity
Triple-mode sensing platform

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Detection of citrate via three modes
Preparation and characterization of CDs and CoFe-LDH
Fluorescence features of CDs and oxidase-mimetic activity of CoFe-LDH
Feasibility of the proposed strategy for citrate detection
Fluorescence and colorimetric sensing of citrate
Determination of citrate via smartphone platform

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors thank the financial support from the Nanjing Polytechnic Institute ( NJPI-RC-2023-08 ). The authors would like to thank Shiyanjia Lab ( www.shiyanjia.com ) for the support of XPS, XRD, TEM, and so on.

The authors thank the financial support from the Nanjing Polytechnic Institute (NJPI-RC-2023-08). The authors would like to thank Shiyanjia Lab (www.shiyanjia.com) for the support of XPS, XRD, TEM, and so on.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The synthesis route (A), XRD (B), TEM images (C-D), UV–vis profiles (E), and FT-IR spectra (F) of CDs and CoFe-LDH.
(A) The survey XPS spectra of CDs and CoFe-LDH. The high-resolution C 1s (B), N 1s (C), and O 1s (D) XPS profiles of CDs, and Fe 2p (E) and Co 2p (F) XPS profiles of CoFe-LDH.

(A) The optimal FL excitation and emission profiles of CDs (Inset: the physical photos under natural and UV light). (B) The FL behavior of CDs under different excitation signals from 320 to 380 nm. (C) UV–vis spectra of CoFe-LDH + TMB, CoFe-LDH, and TMB systems. (D) The EPR spectrum of CoFe-LDH + DMPO system (inset is the mechanism of CoFe-LDH + TMB chromogenic reaction).

(A) Schematic illustration of fluorometric and colorimetric sensing of citrate based on CDs and CoFe-LDH, and the related fluorescence and UV–vis spectra (B-C). (D) The UV–vis absorption spectrum of CoFe-LDH and excitation spectrum of CDs. (E) The FT-IR spectra of CoFe-LDH and CoFe-LDH + Citrate.

(A) The FL spectra of CDs + CoFe-LDH with various levels of citrate, and (B) corresponding linear fitting relationship between F425 and ln[Citrate]. (D) The UV–vis profiles of CDs + CoFe-LDH + TMB with diverse citrate contents, and (E) related fitting curve between A652 and ln[Citrate]. The selectivity of proposed fluorescence (C) and colorimetric (F) approach for citrate analysis.

(A) Schematic diagram of citrate determination via smartphone platform. (B) Linear relationship between red channel value and the logarithm of citrate concentrations (The inset is related physical photo).
Illustration of this triple-mode sensing platform based on CDs + CoFe-LDH system for citrate analysis in urine samples.

head.ce:title: Fluorescence/colorimetry/smartphone triple-mode sensing platform based on carbon dots and Co, Fe-layered double hydroxides for citrate determination

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Life and Health, Nanjing Polytechnic Institute, Nanjing, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Life and Health, Nanjing Polytechnic Institute

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Life and Health, Nanjing Polytechnic Institute, Nanjing, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, School of Science, China Pharmaceutical University, Nanjing 211198, Jiangsu, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemistry, School of Science, China Pharmaceutical University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, School of Science, China Pharmaceutical University, Nanjing 211198, Jiangsu, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Food Science and Engineering, Yangzhou University, Yangzhou 225127, Jiangsu, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Food Science and Engineering, Yangzhou University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Food Science and Engineering, Yangzhou University, Yangzhou, 225127, Jiangsu, China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The cheap, simple, but reliable non-invasive detection means and mature early warning mechanism are beneficial for male population in underdeveloped regions to get rid of the physical and mental pain caused by prostate cancer (PCa). Citrate is presently regarded as a potential biomarker for early non-invasive diagnosis of PCa due to significant changes of citrate levels in urine during the development of PCa, even in the early stages. In this work, the Co, Fe doped layered double hydroxide (CoFe-LDH) can significantly quench the fluorescence of carbon dots (CDs) via inner filter effect (IFE) and catalyze the oxidation of colorless TMB to generate blue TMBox because of excellent oxidase-mimetic activity. However, the introduction of citrate can form a stable complex with CoFe-LDH via intercalation effect, thereby restoring the fluorescence of CDs and inhibition of oxidase-like activity of CoFe-LDH. Based on this, a triple-mode sensing platform (fluorescence, colorimetry, and smartphone) based on CDs + CoFe-LDH system has been fabricated for citrate analysis with correlative detection limits of 0.35, 0.42, and 0.60 μM. Furthermore, these three modes are applied to citrate analysis in male urine samples with ideal recoveries ranging from 94.4 % to 107.4 %, demonstrate the reliability and stability of these triple-mode sensing platform in practical applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Prostate cancer (PCa) is one of the most universal diseases among males worldwide, specifically in western countries [1,2]. Due to the lack of cheap, simple, but reliable non-invasive detection means and mature early warning mechanism, these populations have been exposed to and adapted to this unhealthy lifestyle for a long time, exacerbating their condition and even leading to their death. Therefore, early diagnosis and timely monitoring of PCa are fatal factors in relieving patient pain and reducing mortality rates. Fortunately, numerous studies have found that citrate is highly expressed in normal prostate epithelial cells, but it sharply decreases during the development of PCa and can vary dramatically even in the early stages, thus citrate is presently regarded as a potential biomarker for early non-invasive diagnosis of PCa [3–6].

To date, several regular analytical approaches, including chromatography [7,8], electrochemistry [9,10], colorimetry [11,12], and fluorometry [13,14], have been explored and applied for citrate detection. Nevertheless, the single mode analysis strategy has not met the current requirements of precise analysis owing to its high false positive and false negative rates. Currently, many researchers are committed to developing multimode sensors for more accurate detection of targets [15,16]. For instance, Hou et al. have proposed a novel fluorescence-colorimetric dual mode sensor based on ZTMs and successfully used it for high precision and good selectivity analysis of Cu2+ in water samples [16]. Therefore, ingenious multimodal sensing strategies should be taken seriously and continuously developed with the demand of more accurate noninvasive diagnosis of PCa via citrate detection.

Layered double hydroxide (LDH), a promising two-dimensional layered nanomaterial with large specific surface area, has been widely employed in the fabrication of fluorescent sensors due to its ideal quenching function [17,18]. Recently, some nanomaterials can exhibit excellent enzyme-like activity by doping with active metal elements such as Fe, Co, Mn, and Ni [19–24], among which the LDH is a typical representative [25–27]. Thereby, bifunctional active metal doped LDH with both quenching function and enzyme-mimetic catalytic activity can serve as the core component of multimode optical sensors. In this research, a triple-mode sensing platform (fluorescence, colorimetry, and smartphone) based on CDs + CoFe-LDH system has been fabricated for citrate analysis in urine samples ( Scheme 1). First, the CoFe-LDH synthesized via classic co-precipitation approach possesses excellent FL quenching ability and oxidase-mimetic activity, which can significantly quench the fluorescence of carbon dots (CDs) and catalyze the oxidation of colorless TMB to generate blue TMBox. Nevertheless, the target citrate can partially replace CDs adsorbed on the surface of CoFe-LDH to yield a stable complex through intercalation effect, resulting in the recovery of FL intensity of CDs and inhibition of oxidase-like activity of CoFe-LDH. Hence, an interesting fluorescence mode based on CDs + CoFe-LDH system can be established for citrate analysis. Similarly, a colorimetric sensing mode is ingeniously constructed based on nanozyme catalysis by using TMB as a chromogenic substrate. More importantly, apparent color difference in CDs + CoFe-LDH +TMB + citrate (different contents) system can be captured via the camera function and converted into the color parameters (RGB values) by “Color Grab” app on smartphone. Here, the triple-mode sensing platform (fluorescence, colorimetry, and smartphone modes) was created for early diagnosis of PCa via citrate detection, which provides innovative ideas for design and construction of multimode optical and portable biosensors.

The CDs were prepared by referencing reported literature with slight modifications [28]. Specifically, 1.725 g L-proline was dissolved into 30 mL ultrapure water and then transferred to a 50-mL reactor. After heated at 200 ℃ for 4 h, this brown liquid was purified through filtration with 0.22 µm membranes and dialysis for 6 h utilizing a dialysis membrane (MW = 1000 Da). Finally, the CDs powder was obtained via freeze-drying method.

The CoFe-LDH was synthesized through classic co-precipitation strategy [29]. Typically, 1 mmol Fe(NO3)3·9H2O and 2 mmol Co(NO3)2·6H2O were dissolved in 200 mL ultrapure water. Afterwards, the mixture of NaOH (0.35 mM) and Na2CO3 (0.15 mM) was used to adjust the pH of above solution to 10 under intense stirring. After vigorous stirring at 65 °C for 24 h, the formed precipitates were acquired by filtration and washed with ultrapure water until the supernatant was nearly neutral. Ultimately, the CoFe-LDH was dried at 60 °C for later use.

The fluorescent characteristics of CDs were investigated in this experiment. Specifically, 50 μL CDs (3 mg mL−1) and 950 μL NaAc-HAc buffer (0.2 M, pH 4.0) were mixed into a fluorimeter cuvette and then measured by fluorescence spectrophotometer under various excitation lights.

The oxidase-mimetic activity of CoFe-LDH was explored by selecting TMB as substrate. Specifically, 150 μL CoFe-LDH (1 mg mL−1), 750 μL NaAc-HAc buffer (0.2 M, pH 4.0), and 100 μL TMB (5 mM) were introduced into centrifuge tube in order and reacted for 10 min. Afterwards, the absorbance value of mixture was recorded through UV–vis spectrophotometer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of citrate via three modes
ce:section.ce:para: In a centrifuge tube, 50 μL CDs (3 mg mL−1), 150 μL CoFe-LDH (1 mg mL−1), 100 μL citrate (various concentrations), and 600 μL NaAc-HAc buffer (0.2 M, pH 4.0) were mixed and incubated at room temperature for 3 min. Then the FL signals of above mixed solution were determined by FL spectrophotometer. Next, 100 μL TMB (5 mM) was introduced and reacted for another 10 min. After the photograph of mixed solution was quickly taken with a smartphone, the mixture was scanned from 550 to 750 nm via UV–vis spectrophotometer for colorimetric signals. Eventually, the color information of photograph was converted into R, G, and B parameters via “Color Grab” app.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and characterization of CDs and CoFe-LDH
ce:section.ce:para: The synthesis process of CDs and CoFe-LDH are illustrated in Fig. 1A. By using L-proline as carbon and nitrogen sources, CDs were obtained via a simple hydrothermal reaction. Meanwhile, the CoFe-LDH was synthetized from Fe(NO3)3·9H2O and Co(NO3)2·6 H2O through a facile co-precipitation approach. Then, the morphology and structure of prepared materials were characterized by several means. Similar to the XRD profile of L-proline (Fig. S1), CDs reveal distinct and sharp diﬀraction peaks, suggesting its high crystallinity (Fig. 1B) [30]. Furthermore, a sharp diﬀraction peak appear at 24.14°, which should be attribute to the (002) lattice plane of carbon-based materials [31,32]. As for CoFe-LDH, the XRD profile is completely consistent with reported literatures and the characteristic diffraction peaks at 2θ= 11.71° and 23.43° correspond to (003) and (006) lattice spacings, respectively [33]. The XRD results verify that CDs and CoFe-LDH have been successfully synthetized. Besides, the TEM image of as-prepared CDs reveals spherical particle with an average diameter of approximately 2.33 nm (Fig. S2), and Fig. 1D shows the typical sheet-like morphology of CoFe-LDH. To explore the surface structure of synthesized materials, the relevant UV–vis, FT-IR, and XPS measurements were performed. As displayed in Fig. 1E, both CDs and CoFe-LDH show an extremely wide UV–vis absorption from 200 to 500 nm, meanwhile CDs has a distinct absorption signal at ∼280 nm corresponding to the π→π* of conjugated CC bond [34–36]. In the FT-IR spectrum of CDs (Fig. 1F), the signal at 3420 cm−1 belongs to the O–H stretching vibration, and two peaks at 2970 and 1385 cm−1 are attributed to the C–H stretching and deformation vibration. The characteristic vibration of CO offers a signal at 1620 cm−1, and the absorption band at 1310 cm−1 is assigned to the C–N stretching vibration [37]. The aforesaid FT-IR information confirm CDs are abundant with amino and carboxyl groups. For CoFe-LDH, the wide absorption band at 3405 cm−1 belongs to O–H stretching vibration of surface hydroxyl group and water molecules. A strong signature peak located at 1355 cm−1 originates from carbonate in the interlayer regions of the CoFe-LDH, and the characteristic peak at 490 cm−1 belongs to Fe–O stretching mode [29,38].

In addition, XPS tests were carried out for exploration of surface valence bonds of CDs and CoFe-LDH. In the survey XPS ( Fig. 2A), CDs are composed of C, N, and O elements, and CoFe-LDH contains C, O, Fe and Co elements. The high-resolution C 1 s XPS of CDs (Fig. 2B) indicates the existence of C–C, C–N, C–O, and CO at 284.2, 284.9, 285.7, and 287.3 eV, respectively. The N 1 s profile of CDs (Fig. 2C) consists of three peaks appearing at 398.9 eV (C–N–C), 400.3 eV (N–C), and 401.1 eV (N–H). And the O 1 s peaks at 530.1 and 530.8 eV (Fig. 2D) should be ascribed to C–O/C–OH and CO, respectively [28]. Thus, these results again demonstrate the abundance of amino and carboxyl groups in CDs. Moreover, the Fe 2p peaks (Fig. 2E) should be attributed to Fe2+ 2p3/2, Fe3+ 2p3/2, Fe2+ 2p1/2, and Fe3+ 2p1/2, indicating the presence of multivalent iron (Fe2+ and Fe3+) on the surface of this material [29]. Similarly, the fine Co 2p XPS (Fig. 2F) indicates two major peaks appearing at 780.3 and 796.4 eV assigned to the Co 2p3/2, and Co 2p1/2 species, respectively, along with clear satellite signals, which suggests the existence of Co2+ in the CoFe-LDH structure [39]. Such rich iron and cobalt metal doping endows LDH material excellent enzyme-mimetic activity, which play a critical role in catalysis, analysis, and other fields [40–43].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence features of CDs and oxidase-mimetic activity of CoFe-LDH
ce:section.ce:para: First, the luminescent property of CDs was assessed through related fluorescence tests.

As depicted in Fig. 3A, CDs display optimal excitation and emission peaks located at approximately 330 and 425 nm, respectively, accompanied by bright blue luminescence under 365 nm UV lamp (the inset in Fig. 3A). With the excitation signal away from optimal excitation wavelength, the emission peak gradually shifts and weakens, demonstrating an excitation wavelength-dependent emission characteristic of the CDs (Fig. 3B) [44,45]. Besides, the FL intensity of CDs highly depends on buffer pH, which enhances with the rise of the buffer pH (from pH 3 to pH 9), and then levels off at pH 6–9 (Fig. S3). In contrast, no distinct change on the FL intensity of CDs is observed after continuously irradiated at 365 nm for 60 min (Fig. S4), revealing strong photo-bleaching resistance of CDs. Meanwhile, the luminescence intensity of CDs remains nearly constant during 6 weeks storage (Fig. S5), demonstrating remarkable storage stability of CDs. These superiorities make it play a significant role in the construction of optical sensors.

Next, the TMB was selected as a chromogenic substrate to explore the oxidase-mimetic performance of CoFe-LDH. As illustrated in Fig. 3C, the individual CoFe-LDH or TMB solution is nearly colorless, while a prominent color transition of colorless-to-blue happens when CoFe-LDH and TMB are mingled together, leading to a notable absorption signal at 652 nm. Such a color reaction is ascribed to the oxidation of colorless TMB to its blue intermedium TMBox, indicating the potential oxidase-like activity of CoFe-LDH. To verify that CoFe-LDH indeed acts as a catalyst instead of an oxidant during the color reaction, several common substrates (TMB, OPD, and ABTS) were employed for catalytic reaction in N2- or air-saturated solution. Obviously, the chromogenic reactions of N2-saturated buffers are remarkably suppressed different from those of air-saturated buffers (Fig. S6), implying this catalytic reaction depends on the presence of dissolved oxygen. The outcome testifies that CoFe-LDH indeed severs as an oxidase-mimetic nanozyme.

To further validate the catalytic mechanism of CoFe-LDH, the EPR technique was utilized to investigate the generation of intermediates. According to Fig. 3D, the characteristic signals corresponding to superoxide anion radicals (·O2-) can be detected via the catching agent (DMPO) in the existence of CoFe-LDH. Evidently, CoFe-LDH serves as an oxidase mimetic by first activating dissolved oxygen to yield abundant ·O2- which then induces the oxidation of colorless TMB to blue TMBox (the inset of Fig. 3D). To maximally obtain the oxidase mimicking catalytic performance of CoFe-LDH, the influences of buffer pH and reaction time were studied. As presented in Fig. S7, the catalytic feature of CoFe-LDH highly counts on buffer pH and shows the maximum catalytic activity at pH 4. Fig. S8 describes that the absorption signal (A652) keeps rapidly increasing over time and arrives at dynamic balance within 10 min, thus color reaction time is set as 10 min

Eventually, the corresponding kinetics measurements were conducted to quantitatively assess the oxidase-like catalytic ability of CoFe-LDH. The Michaelis–Menten constant (K
m ) and maximum reaction velocity (V
max ) toward TMB are recorded as 0.09 mM and 2.53 × 10−8 M/s, respectively (Fig. S9). Compared to other oxidase mimics (Table S1), such low K
m implies strong affinity of CoFe-LDH toward substrate TMB. Furthermore, CoFe-LDH exhibits good robustness under harsh conditions (Fig. S10A and Fig. S10B). This superiority can provide CoFe-LDH nanozyme with a great promise in practical applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Feasibility of the proposed strategy for citrate detection
ce:section.ce:para: Interestingly, it was unintentionally found that the introduction of citrate can significantly affect the FL intensity and oxidase-like activity of CoFe-LDH + CDs system. As shown in Fig. 4B, the fluorescence of CDs is greatly quenched by CoFe-LDH, however, once citrate is introduced into the CDs + CoFe-LDH system, the quenched fluorescence of CDs by CoFe-LDH can be effectively partially recovered. Meanwhile, the catalytic chromogenic reaction of TMB is significantly inhibited after the addition of citrate, along with the weakening of UV absorption signal at 652 nm in the CoFe-LDH + CDs + TMB system (Fig. 4C).

To uncover the potential causes for foregoing phenomenon, subsequent proof experiments were implemented. First, the zeta potential of CDs and CoFe-LDH are 12.1 mV and −4.3 mV, respectively (Fig. S11), suggesting that CDs interact with CoFe-LDH via electrostatic interaction. Fig. 4D records the UV–vis absorption profile of CoFe-LDH and the excitation profile of CDs. CoFe-LDH possesses wide absorption band between 200 and 500 nm, which largely overlaps with the FL excitation spectrum of CDs at 330 nm. Consequently, CoFe-LDH induced-fluorescence quenching of CDs may be due to the inner filter effect (IFE). Besides, the FT-IR spectra were utilized to reveal the formation of relatively steady complex between CoFe-LDH and citrate via classic intercalation effect. As proved by Fig. 4E, the characteristic peak of CO (1590 cm−1) exists in the complex of CoFe-LDH and citrate, demonstrating that citrate combines with CoFe-LDH through carboxyl groups [28,46].

In light of the interaction between citrate and CoFe-LDH, the target citrate partially replaces CDs adsorbed on the surface of CoFe-LDH to yield a stable complex through intercalation effect, leading to a visible change in the FL intensity of CDs and the oxidase-like activity of CoFe-LDH. In detail, the fluorescence of CDs can be initially quenched by CoFe-LDH owing to IFE between the two, and subsequently partially recovered owing to the substitution of CDs by citrate. Meanwhile, it is possible that the oxidase mimicking activity of CoFe-LDH is restrained due to the active site of CoFe-LDH occupied or masked by citrate (Fig. 4 A). Thus, the simultaneous variations of visual color and fluorescence offer a feasible pathway to realize synchronous colorimetric and fluorescent detection of citrate.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence and colorimetric sensing of citrate
ce:section.ce:para: Based on the above verifications, an interesting multimodal optical sensor can be constructed for citrate analysis. To achieve better detection performance, some experimental parameters such as CoFe-LDH concentration and reaction time should be optimized. As demonstrated by Fig. S12, the FL intensity of CDs is rapidly quenched and reaches stability when the concentration of CoFe-LDH added is 150 μg mL−1. Thus, 150 μg mL−1 is selected as optimum dosage for following experiments. The reaction time of CDs + CoFe-LDH + Citrate system also influence the analytical performance for the fluorescence determination of citrate. As displayed in Fig. S13, the FL intensity of CDs recovers rapidly and reaches dynamic equilibrium within 3 min, demonstrating that the interaction between citrate and CoFe-LDH is very rapid. Hence, 3 min is chosen as the optimized incubation time for CDs + CoFe-LDH + Citrate system. Furthermore, to investigate the impact of CDs on the oxidase-mimicking activity of CoFe-LDH is extremely necessary. As expected, the typical signal at 652 nm of TMBox is absent in CDs + TMB system, however, the TMB signal in CoFe-LDH + TMB system remains almost unchanged with or without the addition of CDs (Fig. S14). This result suggests that CDs does not affect the oxidase-like catalytic capacity of CoFe-LDH.

Under optimal conditions, the citrate was first determined via fluorescence mode. As demonstrated by Fig. 5A, the fluorescence signal at 425 nm of CDs + CoFe-LDH + Citrate system gradually enhances with the increase of citrate level. The fitting equation is expressed as F425 = 55.87 ln[Citrate] +431.82 (R2 = 0.991) in the ranging from 0.8 to 100 μM (Fig. 5B), and the LOD is calculated to be 0.35 μM according to the S/N = 3. Subsequently, the content of citrate was measured through colorimetric mode after TMB was added and incubated for 10 min. With the rise of citrate level, the absorption peak of CDs + CoFe-LDH + TMB + Citrate system weakens at 652 nm, and the color of this system gradually changes from deep blue to light blue (Fig. 5D). Accordingly, the linear regression equation is A652 = −0.044 ln[Citrate] + 0.419 (R2 = 0.991) in the scope of 0.8–100 μM (Fig. 5E) with the LOD of 0.42 μM. Furthermore, the selectivity of fluorescence and colorimetric strategies toward citrate was studied. The responses of CDs + CoFe-LDH system toward citrate (50 μM) and other possible interfering species (200 μM) were inspected, respectively. Fig. 5 C and Fig. 5F exhibit that these common interferents have a negligible impact on citrate assay, demonstrating the good selectivity of designed fluorescence mode and colorimetric mode for citrate detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of citrate via smartphone platform
ce:section.ce:para: To realize convenient analysis, a smartphone-based platform was constructed for the monitoring of citrate, as illustrated in Fig. 6A. CoFe-LDH possesses the capacity to catalyze the oxidation of colorless TMB into blue TMBox, while the addition of citrate at various concentrations can differentially suppress the catalytic activity of CoFe-LDH, along with these solution systems varying from dark blue to light blue. In view of this, the corresponding photos of detection system with various citrate contents were acquired via camera function and converted into the color parameters (RGB values) by “Color Grab” app on smartphone. Interestingly, there is an acceptable linear relationship between the red channel value (R) and the logarithm of citrate level in the scope of 1–100 μM (Fig. 6B), and the regression equation is [R] = 12.17 ln[Citrate] + 4.44 (R2 = 0.991) with the LOD of 0.60 μM. In comparison with other reported strategies for citrate detection (Table S2), our method displays comparable or even better in terms of linear range and LOD.

To probe the reliability and practicability of proposed triple-mode sensing strategies based on CDs + CoFe-LDH system, the relevant spiking experiments in urine samples were carried out. These urine samples were acquired from healthy male volunteers and their supernatants were collected through centrifugation and filtration, followed by diluting 100-fold with ultrapure water and spiking with given various levels of citrate. Afterwards, citrate contents in the above samples were determined via fluorescent, colorimetric, and smartphone modes and the acquired outcomes are listed in the Table S3. The analysis results of same sample via triple-mode sensing strategies were almost identical, which can avoid erroneous outcomes through cross validation. More importantly, the ideal recoveries ranging from 94.4 % to 107.4 % demonstrate the reliability and stability of these triple-mode sensing strategies in practical applications.

In brief, an ingenious triple-mode sensing platform (fluorescence, colorimetry, and smartphone) based on CDs + CoFe-LDH system has been fabricated for citrate analysis with the linear ranges of 0.8–100 μM, 0.8–100 μM, and 1–100 μM, respectively. What’s more, these three modes have been successfully applied to citrate analysis in male urine samples with ideal recoveries ranging from 94.4 % to 107.4 %, which provides innovative ideas for construction of multimode optical and portable biosensors and early diagnosis of PCa.

Donghang Yin: Methodology, Data Curation, Writing–original draft, Funding acquisition. Shujun Zheng: Formal analysis, Investigation, Methodology, Validation, Writing–original draft. Xuechao Xu: Validation, Writing–Review & Editing.Fei Wang: Visualization, Supervision, Funding acquisition.Linjie Wang: Conceptualization, Data Curation, Supervision, Writing–Review & Editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors thank the financial support from the Nanjing Polytechnic Institute (NJPI-RC-2023-08). The authors would like to thank Shiyanjia Lab (www.shiyanjia.com) for the support of XPS, XRD, TEM, and so on.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.snb.2023.134706.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
Supplementary material.
.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Cancer-associated fibroblast: role in prostate cancer progression to metastatic disease and therapeutic resistance
Cells

Spectrochim. Acta, Part A

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Simultaneous detection of zinc and its pathway metabolites using MALDI MS imaging of prostate tissue

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Donghang Yin graduated from China Pharmaceutical University, and currently he is a lecturer at Nanjing Polytechnic Institute. He shows his interest in biochemical analysis based on nanozymes.
Shujun Zheng is a master candidate under the supervision of Dr. Wang, and she focuses on biochemical analysis based on nanozymes.
Xuechao Xu is a lecturer at Yangzhou University, and he focuses on the analysis of food and biochemistry.

Fei Wang is an associated professor at China Pharmaceutical University. He shows his favor in the fabrication of functional nanomaterials, and is deeply absorbed in the corresponding applications of medical analysis and fluorescence devices.
Linjie Wang is a doctoral candidate at China Pharmaceutical University, and he focuses on the fabrication of sensing systems using nanozymes.
